coredata.dc:title: Synergistic behaviour of silatrane functionalized perylene diimide dye and carbon quantum dots for enhancing photovoltaic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Kalra, Pooja
Surana, Karan
Bhattacharya, Bhaskar
Singh, Gurjaspreet
Durga, Geeta
coredata.dc:description: Perylene based dyes have been explored for their exceptional optical and conducting properties for the past few decades and have found applications in organic electronics. In recent years, quantum dots (QD) derived from carbonaceous materials have gained prominence for their multidisciplinary applications. In the present work, we have proposed a novel architecture of a Dye Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) in which the photo-anodic layer of mesoporous TiO2 is co-sensitized with Carbon QD followed by a novel perylene diimide (PDI) sensitizer having silatrane anchor group (PDIST). The structure of the synthesized silatrane-ended PDI was characterized using spectroscopic techniques. Further, the structure was optimized using the DFT-B3LYP/6–31G level of theory. The fabricated DSSC showed eleven folds increase in efficiency when compared to only PDIST sensitized solar cell which concludes the synergistic interaction of CQD and PDIST dye. Owing to such remarkable properties, this setup of co-sensitization may open up new avenues in the realm of sensitized solar cells.

Silatranes
Photoluminescence
Quantum yield
Electrochemical properties
Dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady state absorption and emission characteristics
Synthesis of silatrane functionalized perylene diimide (PDIST)
Fabrication of dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC)
Synthesis, structural characterization and optimization of PDIST

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author KS is grateful to UGC, India for providing the UGC-DS Kothari Postdoc Fellowship (No. F. 4-2/2006 (BSR)/CH/20-21/0247). Financial assistance as Incentive to Senior Faculties (to BB) under the Institute of Eminence Scheme (Dev. Scheme No. 6031) from Banaras Hindu University is gratefully acknowledged. The authors warmly appreciate the support provided by CIF of Jamia Millia Islamia to carry out Fluorescence, DSC and DTA/TGA measurements and CDRI Lucknow for NMR and Mass characterizations. The author GD is grateful to Sharda University for providing all the support in order to complete the synthesis and characterization work.

The author KS is grateful to UGC, India for providing the UGC-DS Kothari Postdoc Fellowship (No. F. 4-2/2006 (BSR)/CH/20-21/0247). Financial assistance as Incentive to Senior Faculties (to BB) under the Institute of Eminence Scheme (Dev. Scheme No. 6031) from Banaras Hindu University is gratefully acknowledged. The authors warmly appreciate the support provided by CIF of Jamia Millia Islamia to carry out Fluorescence, DSC and DTA/TGA measurements and CDRI Lucknow for NMR and Mass characterizations. The author GD is grateful to Sharda University for providing all the support in order to complete the synthesis and characterization work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HOMO and LUMO energy levels and electron density as calculated by DFT, (b) and (c) Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surface of PDIST.
(a) UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the PDIST. (Inset) Tauc's plot of the dye. (b) Picture of PDIST under daylight (DL) and under 365 nm UV light.
Time-Resolved PL analysis of the PDIST. (Inset) Shown in linear scale.
CV analysis of the PDIST dye.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of TiO2 coated FTO electrode at various stages.
J-V analysis of the solar cells.
Schematic diagram of the charge transfer phenomenon.
Impedance analysis of the solar cells under illumination. (Inset) equivalent circuit used for curve fitting.
Synthesis of Silatrane Functionalized Perylene Diimide (PDIST).
Energies of different HOMO and LUMO orbitals as calculated by DFT.
Photovoltaic and impedance parameters of the solar cells.
A comparative study of devices employing perylene dye or Carbon QD as sensitizer.

row.entry: Carbon QD (Citric acid, uric acid) and Perylene dye

head.ce:title: Synergistic behaviour of silatrane functionalized perylene diimide dye and carbon quantum dots for enhancing photovoltaic performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perylene based dyes have been explored for their exceptional optical and conducting properties for the past few decades and have found applications in organic electronics. In recent years, quantum dots (QD) derived from carbonaceous materials have gained prominence for their multidisciplinary applications. In the present work, we have proposed a novel architecture of a Dye Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) in which the photo-anodic layer of mesoporous TiO2 is co-sensitized with Carbon QD followed by a novel perylene diimide (PDI) sensitizer having silatrane anchor group (PDIST). The structure of the synthesized silatrane-ended PDI was characterized using spectroscopic techniques. Further, the structure was optimized using the DFT-B3LYP/6–31G level of theory. The fabricated DSSC showed eleven folds increase in efficiency when compared to only PDIST sensitized solar cell which concludes the synergistic interaction of CQD and PDIST dye. Owing to such remarkable properties, this setup of co-sensitization may open up new avenues in the realm of sensitized solar cells.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perylene diimide (PDI), a well-documented fluorophore, has been widely utilized in organic electronics as a typical n-type organic semiconductor [1]. The perylene core has attracted the attention of researchers owing to its excellent properties such as high photostability, redox potential, excellent optoelectronic properties, and high quantum yield. The perylene dyes have been largely used as a light-absorbing medium in both the Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) [2] and Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSC) [3]. The light-absorbing properties of these molecules can be fine-tuned by tailoring the architecture at the two positions of the perylene core i.e., peri and bay [4–6].

The experiments performed by Burfeindt et al. [7] dated back in 1996, explored the ability of electron transfer from the excited state of perylene dye to TiO2, and have opened new avenues of development of perylene-based DSSC. The authors suggested three major advantages of using perylene dyes in DSSC are: (a) well-separated absorption spectra of the oxidized state and the first excited singlet state (S1); (b) the decay of excited perylene monomer occurs only radiatively; (c) the oxidation potential of the first excited singlet state i.e., the energy of the donor state, lies sufficiently above the lower energy contour of the conduction band of many wide bandgap oxide semiconductors, which would allow the charge transfer. The efficiency of DSSC utilizing perylene diimide sensitizers can be tuned by tailoring the structure of the perylene core and anchoring groups. Perylene imides can be easily functionalized with carboxylic acid, phosphonic acid, or anhydride groups, which serve as anchors for attachment onto inorganic semiconductor surfaces [8–10]. The first report of PDIs to be used as photovoltaic sensitizers referred the anchoring of carboxylic acid groups onto 2.5 µm thick nanoporous SnO2 on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass. The fabricated DSSC showed a maximum IPCE of ∼ 30% (wavelength region 458–488 nm) and an overall efficiency of 0.89% [11]. A plethora of reports has been documented utilizing carboxylic acid anchor groups with wide variation in the structure at peri and bay positions adsorbed onto TiO2 nanocrystalline films [12–14]. The modifications in the nanocrystalline TiO2 film such as bromine-doping further improved the maximum IPCE to 40% in 440–530 nm wavelength region [14]. The perylene monoimide with one anhydride anchor group in the sensitizers has been synthesized by various research groups and their corresponding photovoltaic performance has been documented [15,16]. Of late a new group, silatrane (triethanolamine protected siloxanes) has been introduced by Brennan et.al. in the porphyrin sensitizers to anchor onto TiO2 [17]. It has been hypothesized by the authors that siloxane undergoes deprotection and forms siloxyl linkages (proposed Si-O-Ti) which were highly resistant to hydrolysis under various examined conditions. The same could be beneficial in enhancing the long-term stability of DSSCs, especially for aqueous electrolyte-based photoelectrochemical cells. Quite recently, Myong et al. utilized a silatrane moiety on one side of PDI and incorporated it into an anodic aluminium oxide membrane and studied its aggregation behaviour by varying the solvent polarity [18].

In search of alternate eco-friendly materials for energy production, scientists stumbled upon yet another form of Carbon, called Carbon QD or Graphene QD [19,20]. These nanodots are colourful forms of Carbon exhibiting strong absorption and fluorescence characteristics. They are being actively pursued owing to their biocompatibility, wide availability of precursors, ease of synthesis methods, tunable surface functionalities, chemical inertness, controllable size, and diverse applications [21]. Their emission can be both excitation wavelength-dependant or independent, which depends solely on the surface groups present. Consequently, they have shown exciting results in the field of sensing (chemical and optical), photovoltaics, photocatalysis, LED, bioimaging, and others [22–25]. An innovative Perovskite Solar Cell (PSC) was reported by Zou et al. where CQD was immersed within the perovskite layer. The optimized incorporation of CQD yielded an efficiency of 7.62% while the device without CQD showed an efficiency of 4.4% only [25]. Chava et al. reported CQD sensitized ZnO which showed a maximum efficiency of 1.18% with iodine-based electrolyte [26]. A recent effort by our group showed that sensitizing the TiO2 layer with CQD prior to N719 dye resulted in better device characteristics. The CQD incorporated device showed an efficiency of 0.54% while the bare N719 sensitized device yielded only 0.22% [22]. Owing to the band structure of CQD and its absorption characteristics, it exhibits better characteristics when utilized as a co-sensitizer rather than being the sole sensitizer, although there was no sign of any synergistic interaction between dye and CQD. A study presented by Bora et al. employed TiO2 sensitized with N719 dye followed by sensitization with C QD [44]. The change in η of the devices was not so dramatic; the TiO2 N719 dye device showed η of 6.28% while the TiO2 N719 dye-C QD device resulted in η of 6.90%. In a recent report by Efa et al. the authors had fabricated DSSC using ZnO nanoparticles embedded with and without C QD and sensitized with N719 dye [45]. The best performing device with N719 dye sensitized ZnO resulted in a η of 2.90% while the C QD embedded ZnO sensitized with N719 dye showed η of 5.92%. The effect of using C QD in enhancing the efficiency was clearly established. In another study by the same group, Yanagida et al. reported similar study with NiO and obtained roughly four times enhancement in efficiency. Furthermore, the authors indicated that it was the synergetic effect caused by the co-adsorption of C QD and N719 dye and not simply an additive one [46].

The basic architecture and working mechanism of a sensitized type photovoltaic device is explained in a previous paper [27]. Since dye and QD both have their individual strengths; hence, it could be possible to utilize their strengths in synergy for fabricating better and efficient dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC). However, the vital thing to check before co-sensitization is the proper band-alignment of the chosen materials with respect to the wide band-gap semiconductor in order to have a smooth transition of charges [28]. In view of the above findings, herein, we report the fabrication of DSSC where we have used a novel PDI derivative bearing silatrane anchoring units on both sides (abbr. as PDIST) as a sensitizer. The synthesized dye was found to have solubility in a variety of solvents, showed excellent absorption characteristics, and generated high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), thereby demonstrating its applicability as a sensitizer in DSSC. The structure of PDIST was characterized using spectroscopic techniques and was optimized using the DFT-B3LYP/6–31 G level of theory. The CQD was prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid as the precursor and uric acid for nitrogen doping/surface passivation [22]. The dye and QD were utilized to fabricate a DSSC, which exhibited encouraging results. The fabricated co-sensitized device exhibited eleven folds higher efficiency than the device sensitized with only dye. This approach for the fabrication of a co-sensitized DSSC using Perylene based dye along with CQD is not yet reported to the best of our knowledge.

All the chemicals used to synthesize PDIST were of reagent grade. Standard methods were used to purify/dry the solvents and distilled them freshly prior to use [29]. Briefly, the reagent grade chemicals used were Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA, Sigma Aldrich), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (Merck), Triethanolamine (Merck), KOH pellets (Merck), dichloromethane (DCM, Merck), diethyl ether (Merck), toluene (Merck), uric acid (Alfa Aesar), and citric acid (Qualikems). Freshly prepared double distilled (DD) water was used wherever required.

The elemental (CHN) analysis was carried out on a Euro Vector E-3000 Elemental Analyser. The Electrospray Ionization Mass Spectrometry (ESI-MS) of the synthesized molecule was recorded using Waters Alliance e2695/HPLC-TQD mass spectrometer. The Infrared spectrum was acquired by Agilent Technology Cary 630 FTIR in the spectral range from 4000 to 650 cm−1. The 1H NMR spectrum was recorded on a Bruker Avance 400/AvIII HD-300 (FT NMR) spectrometer in a mixture of DMSO and CDCl3 and the chemical shifts were recorded with reference to TMS as an internal reference. The zeta potential was obtained from Horiba SZ100 Z at a working potential of 3.3 V. The molecular structure of the well characterized PDIST was optimised for its energy minima and later the geometrical parameters were computed with the Gaussian 16 software package program [30]. In theoretical studies, 6–31 G (d,p) was selected as a basic set due to the standard theory level for C, H, N, S, and F elements. Furthermore, the Density Functional Theory (DFT) method, named Becke, 3-parameter, Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) was selected as a method to interpret Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) analysis in their optimized structures. All optimizations were performed with tight convergence criteria without any symmetry constraints [31].

To investigate the thermal behaviour of PDIST, Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA)/Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) were carried out using DSC (Setaram, LABSYS DSC 131, 700 °C) and TGA/DTA (Setaram, LABSYS EVO 1150 °C) instruments, respectively. DSC scans were acquired by heating the samples from room temperature to 300 °C whereas TGA/DTA scans were obtained by heating the sample from room temperature to 800 °C. All the analyses were performed at a heating rate of 10 °C under nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state absorption and emission characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption characteristics of PDIST dye were analyzed in the range of 250–700 nm using a Shimadzu UV1800 spectrophotometer at room temperature. For the analysis, 40 µL of the dye stock solution was diluted with dichloromethane (DCM) and sonicated for 5 min in a quartz cuvette. The energy-gap analysis was carried out by Tauc's equation [32,33]. Further, the absorption studies of the FTO glass electrode at various stages of TiO2 coating and sensitization were carried out from 300 to 700 nm.

The photoluminescence (PL) measurements were accomplished using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer in a cuvette of 1 cm path length with 2.5 mm of excitation and emission slit width. All the measurements were executed at room temperature.

The PLQY (λex = 523 nm) of PDIST in DCM was estimated relative to Rhodamine 6 G (φf = 0.95, in methanol) by using the following equation (i).
(i)
Φ
P D I S T
=
Φ
R
(
I
P D I S T
I R
)
(
η
P D I S T
2
η R 2
)
(
A R
A
P D I S T
)
Where φ is the Quantum Yield (QY), I represent the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript ‘R’ stands for reference and ‘PDIST’ stands for PDIST molecule in solution [31]. Time Resolved Fluorescence Lifetime Measurement Spectrometer (Horiba, DeltaFlex-01-DD) was used to measure the lifetime of excited PDIST fluorophore. The absolute PLQY (λex = 523 nm) of PDIST in DCM was also measured using the integrating sphere accessory of FS5 Spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh) to corroborate the calculated PLQY.

The electrochemical investigations of the materials and devices were characterized using CHI 604D electrochemical workstation. The electrochemical performance of the dye was assessed by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique. The scanning was performed in the range of +3.5–-2.5 V at a scan rate of 0.05 V/s using a three-electrode system where Ag/AgCl served as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and PDIST dye-coated FTO glass served as the working electrode. The electrolyte used here was 0.1 M KI and 0.01 M I2 dissolved in 20 mL of acetonitrile. The J-V characteristics of the devices were measured using a solar simulator having a Xenon lamp operated at 150 W with AM 1.5 G filter. The devices were scanned in the voltage range -1 V to +1 V at a scan rate of 0.5 V/s. An area of 0.30 cm2 was exposed to the light. The AC impedance analysis of the devices was carried out under 1 sun illumination in the frequency range 100k Hz to 0.1 Hz at an applied bias equal to the open-circuit voltage (Voc) of the device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silatrane functionalized perylene diimide (PDIST)

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of 1-(3-Aminopropyl) silatrane was carried out implying the method reported elsewhere in the literature [34]. A 1:1 M ratio of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane with triethanolamine was taken in a round bottom flask fitted on a Schlenk line along with a catalytic amount of KOH and kept for stirring at 60 °C for 3 h. The structure was established by elemental analyses, NMR, IR, and Mass spectrometric techniques and are reported in our previous publication [35].

PDIST was synthesized following a method similar to our previous publication [31]. Perylene tetracarboxylic acid dianhydride (PTCDA, 0.500 g, 1.27 mM) in DMF (10 mL) was taken into a 100 mL round bottom flask fitted with dean stark apparatus and heated at 140 °C with constant stirring. 1-(3-Aminopropyl) silatrane, (0.592 mg, 2.54 mM) was then added to the reaction vessel and the resultant mixture was stirred for 5 h at 140 °C and was cooled upon completion. The silatrane-based perylene diimide (PDIST) was recovered by pouring the reaction mixture into ice-cold water. The bright red precipitate thus formed was retrieved by filtration, washed several times with de-ionized water, and dried in a vacuum. The crude product thus obtained was recrystallized from DCM.

PDIST: Colour and state: Dark red crystalline powder; Yield: 80.75%; Anal. Calcd. For C42H44N4O10Si2 (FW= 820.99058) (%): C, 61.44; H, 5.40; N, 6.82; O, 19.49; Si, 6.84. Found (%): C, 61.40; H, 5.43; N, 6.79; O, 19.51; Si, 6.86. ESIMS (m/z) (cf. Figure S1, supplementary information (SI)): 819[M]+, 1099, 1284, 1830, 789, 735, 718, 624, 426, and 387. FTIR (cm−1) (cf. Figure S2, SI.): 1689, 1652, 1342, 1100, 1019, 814. 1H NMR (400 MHz, δ, ppm, CDCl3) (cf. Figure S3, SI.): δ 8.66 ppm (d, ArH), 8.63 (d, ArH), 3.76 (t, CH2- O- Si), 2.8 ppm (t, NCH2), 2.62 (t, CH2 N-Si), 1.68 (m, CH2), 0.9 (t, Si-CH2).

The synthesis of CQD is provided in our previous work [22]. In a typical process, citric acid and uric acid were mixed in the ratio of 1:5 in 60 mL DD water at ∼ 60 °C. The pH of the resultant solution was brought to neutral and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was performed for 5 h @ 175 °C in a laboratory oven. The obtained transparent, slightly yellowish solution was filtered through syringe filter twice to collect narrow-size dispersed CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC)
ce:section.ce:para: The initial cleaning process of Fluorine doped Tin Oxide (FTO, Sigma Aldrich, ∼ 7 Ω/sq) glass along with the coating of titania blocking layer on the working electrode and platinum solution on the counter electrode was followed exactly as reported previously [36]. Transparent nano TiO2 paste (Sigma Aldrich) was applied on the working electrode by the doctor blade method. The electrode was sintered at 500 °C for half an hour. For sensitization, the working electrode was dipped in CQD or PDIST solution under continuous stirring for 6 – 8 h, which was followed by washing the electrode gently with a stream of acetone (to remove any unabsorbed material). PEO-based polymer electrolyte was used with iodine redox couples in a two-step coating method as described previously [22]. Finally, a Pt counter electrode was placed on top for completing the sandwich structure. Essentially, three sensitized solar cells were prepared: CD1, TiO2 sensitized with only CQD; PD1, TiO2 sensitized with only PDIST; and PD2, TiO2 sequentially sensitized with CQD followed by PDIST.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, structural characterization and optimization of PDIST
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of PDIST was carried out in a dean stark apparatus by reacting perylene tetracarboxylic acid dianhydride and silatrane in 1:2 molar ratio (Scheme 1) at 140 °C. The reaction resulted in a dark red colored precipitate with a good yield (80.75%). Imidization was completed in 5 h. PDIST was found to be soluble in almost all polar/non-polar solvents (DMSO, DMF, C6H6, etc.). The analytical data suggest that two moles of silatrane reacted with a mole of perylene tetracarboxylic acid dianhydride with quantitative separation of the water molecules.

The ESIMS (m/z) 820.2 [M +] value represents the intact PDIST molecule, minus one electron removed by the impacting beam. Furthermore, the fragments of PDIST due to the release of neutral molecules or free radicals occurred at 789, 735, 718, 624, 426, and 387, with the base peak for the imide unit occurring at 388. The arbitrary intensity value of 100 is assigned to the base peak at 388, while the other peaks are given their proportionate value. Also, the peaks representing the aggregated molecules at higher m/z values were also obtained. (cf. Fig. S1, SI). The FTIR spectrum (cf. Fig. S2, SI) of PDIST revealed the disappearance of the anhydride peak and the presence of typical peaks of disubstituted Perylene diimide at 1689 and 1652 cm−1 assigned to the carbonyl groups of imides, indicating a successful imidization reaction on the perylene dianhydride structure. 1H NMR spectroscopy (400 MHz, mixture solvent CDCl3+DMSO) showed the set of signals with relative areas from left to right at δ 8.66 ppm (d, ArH), 8.63 (d, ArH), 3.76 (t, CH2- O- Si), 2.8 ppm (t, NCH2), 2.62 (t, CH2 N-Si), 1.68 (m, CH2), 0.9 (t, Si-CH2) affirming the structure of PDIST. The peaks of solvents were seen at their usual position. (cf. Figure S3, SI)

The optimized structure of PDIST was obtained at the minimum potential energy using 631-G basis sets of B3LYP theory (cf. Fig. S4, SI). All other theoretical global chemical reactivity parameters were calculated at the minimum energy optimization as indicated in Table 1 . The energy gap between HOMO-LUMO and other important key factors for molecular electron conductivity such as hardness (η), softness (σ), chemical potential (µ), global electronegativity (χ) and electrophilicity index (ω) were calculated using Koopman's theorem for closed-shell molecules [37]. Here ‘I’ represents the ionization potential and ‘A’ is the electron affinity of the molecule, and both of them can directly be evaluated using orbital energies of HOMO and LUMO as given below.
I = − E
HOMO
and
A = − E
LUMO

Then other parameters are analysed by given equations
Hardness
(
η
)
=
( I − A )
/ 2
Chemical
potential
(
μ
)
= −
( I + A )
/ 2
Softness
( S )
= 1 / 2
η
Electro
negativity
(
χ
)
=
( I + A )
/ 2
Electrophilicity
index
(
ω
)
=
μ
2
/ 2
η

The energy values for different HOMO and LUMO orbitals are summarized in Table 2 and energy densities are exhibited in Fig. 1 (a). The red and green colour of the orbital represents the positive and negative phases accordingly. Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) is useful to visualize both yellow and blue regions indicating the electrophilic and nucleophilic regions, respectively indicated in Fig. 1(b and c).

Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) measurement was carried out to understand the occurrence of energy changes associated with physical/chemical processes taken place while heating the PDIST dye. Fig. S5a (SI) revealed no obvious phase transitions due to melting or changing crystal structures while heating the dye from room temperature up to 300 °C. The similar results could also be observed in the DTA scans (Fig. S5b, SI) up to 400 °C. However, a broad exothermic peak (Ti = 463, Tonset = 503 and Tfinal = 734) was observed post degradation temperature, which may be attributed to the formation of Si-O-Si linkages. The Thermogravimetric (TG) scans (Fig. S5c, SI) indicated excellent thermal stability as it started weight loss after 300 °C and exhibited 5% weight loss at 349 °C which is well congruous with the results reported in literature [38]. The obtained results were attributed to strong intermolecular π–π interactions between the stacked perylene cores. From the DSC and TGA/DTA measurements, it can be concluded that the dye can be safely utilized in DSSC without the fear of degradation or phase transitions.

The analysis of absorption and band-gap is essential to understand the optical characteristics of a dye. The absorption characteristics of PDIST are shown in Fig. 2 a. The dye starts to absorb the incident photons from ∼ 620 nm onwards and gives its first signature sharp absorption peak at 524 nm. Secondary and tertiary absorption wavelength maxima (λmax) with gradually lowered intensities are observed at 487 nm and 457 nm, respectively. Further, two unresolved absorption humps are visible between 300 and 400 nm leading to a final absorption peak in the UV region at 260 nm. The narrow absorption peaks between 450 nm to 550 nm having a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ∼ 15 nm suggest the formation of uniform structured particles of high purity. The absorption in the UV region (both short-range and long-range) is consistent with its luminescence under UV light (Fig. 2b) where it shows bright yellow colour.

The energy gap of the dye was calculated according to Tauc's equation and is shown in the inset of Fig. 2 [32]. The calculated energy gap was ∼ 2.31 eV which corroborates the data obtained from the DFT calculation. The difference between the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO deduced from the DFT calculation was ∼2.31 eV. Hence, the absorption characteristics and energy gap does support its application as a sensitizer for solar cells.

The characterizations of CQD are detailed in our previous article [22]. The TEM image and absorption spectra are shown in supplementary information (Figure S6). The zeta potential of the CQD was obtained to be -150.3 mV, which confirms their excellent stability (Figure S7, SI).

The steady-state emission characteristics of the dye excited at 520 nm are shown in Fig. 2. The emission spectrum is practically the mirror image of the absorption curve which is consistent with the emission nature of perylene-based dyes [39]. Further, it is to be noted that the almost mirror image emission spectrum occurs due to the transition of vibrational energy state from S0 to S1 state, i.e., the singlet ground state to the first electronic state. In such electronic excitation, there is no alteration in the nuclear geometry, hence the energy level spacing of the vibrational states is more or less equal to that of the ground state, thereby resulting in symmetrical absorption and emission nature. The three peaks with gradually decreasing intensities appear at 537 nm, 573 nm, and 620 nm, respectively. The narrow sharp emission peaks of FWHM ∼ 15 nm reflect the purity and excellent luminescent nature of the prepared dye. A stokes shift of merely 14.3 nm was observed. This is also consistent with the absorption and luminescence observed in daylight and UV light, respectively (Fig. 2b).

It is important to analyse the fluorescence lifetime since it determines the time available for a fluorophore before it interacts with its environment or decays out. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed at an excitation wavelength of 520 nm and an emission wavelength of 537 nm to investigate the lifetime of excited PDIST. The analysis of fluorescence data was done using iterative reconvolution, performed using EzTime-DAM Software to describe a mono-exponential or multi-exponential decay convolved with the instrument response function (IRF). We determined the goodness of fit by looking at the randomness of weighted residuals and the value of the parameter chi-square (χ2). The fluorescence decay of PDIST was fitted as a tri-exponential decay function with the fastest component having a lifetime of 0.29 ns and the slowest component having 4.18 ns. The results obtained are summarised in Table S1 and the logarithmic decay curve is exhibited in Fig. 3 (Inset: linear scale) Fig. 4 .

The Photoluminescent Quantum Yield (PLQY) as estimated in reference to Rhodamine-6 G was found to be as high as 80% which is well consistent with the reported value due to perylene based fluorophores [31,40]. The results thus obtained were well corroborated with the measured absolute quantum yield with a value of 81.00% (Fig. S8).

The assessment of redox potential was conducted using CV measurement. The first oxidation hump appears at 1.86 V followed by a closely placed clear peak at 2.25 V. On the other hand, the corresponding reduction peaks appear at 1.78 V and −2.02 V. The nature of observed CV is similar to the ones reported in the literature as there is no activity between - 0.5 V and + 0.5 V [41]. Furthermore, the presence of both oxidation and reduction peaks affirms that the reaction is reversible, which asserts its candidature as a viable sensitizer for DSSC. Moreover, the two cycles are almost perfectly overlapping, which asserts is repeatable nature.

The UV–Vis spectra of the working electrodes were performed to understand the absorption effects of CQD and PDIST sensitization on TiO2, which would help us understand the impact of co-sensitization, if any. The TiO2 coated electrode shows a continuous rise in absorption and attains a λmax at ∼ 340 nm which is consistent with its absorption nature (Fig. 5 ). The absorption trend of the TiO2 / CQD coated electrode overlaps almost perfectly with that of the only TiO2 coated electrode and shows a comparatively narrow λmax with a slight shifting of the peak towards lower wavelength. This is consistent with the absorption characteristics of CQD since it starts showing absorption beyond 350 nm. Although the prepared CQD showed clear absorption between 200 and 300 nm [22], the use of glass substrate restricted those observations. Nevertheless, the shift in absorption trend of TiO2 / CQD electrode beyond 400 nm is visible. The addition of PDIST to the electrode introduced a significant increment in the absorption intensity with minor humps appearing around 500 nm, corresponding to the absorption by PDIST. The electrode shows further enhancement in absorption beyond 400 nm and the intensity is almost doubled compared to the TiO2 / CQD electrode. It should be noted that PDIST shows only slight absorption in 300–400 nm (Fig. 2), hence, the doubling in absorption intensity beyond 400 nm corresponds to a synergistic interaction between CQD and PDIST. If we consider the case where CQD and PDIST were absorbing individually without any interaction, a substantial rise in absorption intensity beyond 400 nm would not have occurred. Since, the inclusion of PDIST allowed the absorption of visible light along with enhancement in the absorption of UV light, the observed absorption characteristics confirm a definite synergistic interaction between CQD and PDIST. Overall, the TiO2 / CQD / PDIST absorption trend is suggestive of sufficient photon absorption which should be useful in generating decent photovoltaic performance.

An assessment of the photovoltaic parameters of the prepared devices would reveal their performance. The current density vs. voltage plot of the devices under 1 sun illumination is shown in Fig. 6 . The device sensitized with only PDIST dye, PD1 generates a poor open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.36 V with a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 0.50 mA/cm2. This resulted in an efficiency (η) of only 0.10%. A below-average fill factor (FF) of 0.55 also aided in the poor device performance. The low Voc could be attributed to the poor band-alignment, which also resulted in enhanced recombination, thus leading to poor current. The device sensitized with only CQD, CD1 showed comparatively better performance by generating a Voc of 0.55 V and a Jsc of 1.47 mA/cm2. However, it lacked greatly in FF which signifies poor interaction between the formed junctions of the device. On the other hand, the device PD2, having the advantage of co-sensitization showed an enhancement in all the parameters. The Voc improved marginally to 0.58 V from 0.55 V while the Jsc was enhanced by five times. This led to an overall increment in the device η to 1.10%. A considerable effect of ion polarization is visible in the device which is unavoidable due to the use of quasi-solid polymer electrolyte [42]. The obtained photovoltaic parameters are tabulated in Table 3 .

Essentially, PD2 generated an efficiency eleven times greater than PD1, which effectively shows the synergistic effect of CQD with PDIST dye. The device characteristics are improved greatly in contrast to the devices where the sensitizers are used separately. The reason for the increased current density could be attributed to the increased photon trapping capability, owing to the coverage of a wider region of the UV–Vis spectrum. Furthermore, the introduction of CQD between dye and TiO2 layer would have aided in better charge separation and transfer of generated electrons thereby reducing the effective recombination rate. The reason for enhanced Voc of PD2 compared to PD1 could be simply attributed to the improvement in the overall band-alignment of the device [43].
Although, the architecture of the devices presented in both the studies are different from our study, the authors have had similar observations, which cements our inference of synergetic phenomenon.

Considering the strong absorption nature of the dye between 450 - 550 nm the photon absorption should have been higher, which would have resulted in higher output current; however, such was not the case with PD1. The reason could have been the difference in the LUMO energy level of PDIST and TiO2, which aided in recombination rather than charge transfer into the external circuit. In the co-sensitized solar cell, i.e., PD2, the addition of CQD facilitated the incorporation of an intermittent LUMO level, thereby helping the electron transfer from the dye to TiO2. The schematic diagram of the electron transfer is shown in Fig. 7
[47].

The sole CQD sensitized device could generate low photocurrent owing to its limited coverage of the UV–Vis spectrum. Thus, the synergistic effect of CQD and PDIST is reflected in the enhancement of photovoltaic parameters. In addition to generating more excitons, CQD also helped significantly in transferring the excited electrons from dye to TiO2 which led to eleven times increase in the efficiency of PD2. Such an unprecedented enhancement in solar cell parameters has not been reported previously in this configuration. A brief comparative study of DSSC employing perylene dye or CQD are given below in Table 4 .

The prepared devices were subjected to electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for understanding the interfacial impedance. The obtained Cole-Cole plot was subjected to curve fitting as per the equivalent circuit and the final plot is shown in Fig. 8 . The deployed equivalent circuit is shown in the inset of Fig. 8 and the obtained parameters are tabulated in Table 3 where Rs represents the external impedance due to the wired connections, FTO, etc., Rt is the impedance occurring at the junction of the counter electrode, and polymer electrolyte and Rc gives the impedance values at the junction of the working electrode and polymer electrolyte [36]. Interestingly, the impedance analysis plot confirms the observations from J-V analysis, wherein a decrement in the Rc values can be observed in the order PD2 > CD1 > PD1. The Rc value of PD2 is approximately 2.5 times lower than that of PD1 signifying the improved interaction of the working electrode with the electrolyte (Table 3). This further affirms the lowering of charge recombination in PD2 compared to PD1. Furthermore, the lowered interfacial impedance resulted in better charge extraction, thereby improving the photovoltaic parameters of the device.

We have reported a synergistic approach to fabricate a perylene dye and CQD based co-sensitized DSSC which showed an enhanced power conversion efficiency (PCE) as compared to DSSC sensitized with only perylene diimide dye or CQD. The synergistic effect was well evident from the UV–Vis absorption studies and the J-V analysis. Herein, we worked on the silatrane anchoring groups that attached onto TiO2 well and CQD as co-sensitizer helped in generating more excitons while aiding in efficient electron transfer. Amino silatrane groups were introduced at peri positions of perylene dianhydride and the spectroscopic techniques confirmed its structure. Furthermore, the structure of synthesized molecule was optimized using the DFT-B3LYP/6–31 G level of theory to understand its electronic properties. The dye exhibited excellent absorption and emission characteristics along with a high absolute quantum yield of 81.00% (80% calculated). The co-sensitized device showed an improvement in the overall photovoltaic parameters along with reduction in the junction impedance and a net eleven folds enhancement in efficiency. This potential synergistic interaction of PDIST dye and CQD could reveal more fascinating results and the study is currently underway.

Pooja Kalra: Methodology, Software, Validation. Karan Surana: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Bhaskar Bhattacharya: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Gurjaspreet Singh: Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision. Geeta Durga: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Financial assistance as Incentive to Senior Faculties (to BB) under the Institute of Eminence Scheme (Dev. Scheme No. 6031) from Banaras Hindu University is gratefully acknowledged. The authors warmly appreciate the support provided by CIF of Jamia Millia Islamia to carry out Fluorescence, DSC and DTA/TGA measurements and CDRI Lucknow for NMR and Mass characterizations. The author GD is grateful to Sharda University for providing all the support in order to complete the synthesis and characterization work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.135470.
